<think>
Okay, so I'm trying to figure out whether the mutant code change in DateTimeZone.java caused a fault adjacent situation. Let me start by understanding what's happening here.

The user provided some information about failing tests and the changes made to the code. The main issue seems to be with the getOffset method in DateTimeZone. Specifically, lines 326-331 were modified. 

Looking at the mutant code changes:

Lines 326-330:
Original code was:
public final int getOffset(long instant) {
    if (instant == null) {
        return printOffset(getOffset(DateTimeUtils.currentTimeMillis()));
    }
    return printOffset(getOffset(instant.getMillis()));
}

But it looks like there were some changes. The exact lines after the change are a bit unclear, but from what I can gather, line 329 was modified to have an 'if (false)' condition.

So in the mutant code, line 329 is changed to:
if (false) {

This means that the condition inside the if statement will always be false. So the block inside this if won't execute. Therefore, the method now just returns getOffset(instant.getMillis()), regardless of whether instant is null or not.

Wait, no. Let me think again. The original code had an 'if (instant == null)'. So when instant was null, it would call printOffset(getOffset(current time)), else it used the instant's millis. 

In the mutant version, line 329 is changed to if (false), so that condition is always false. Therefore, the code inside the if block doesn't run. Then, after that, there's a return statement at line 330 which returns getOffset(instant.getMillis()). So regardless of whether instant was null or not, it now uses the instant's millis.

But wait, in the original code, when instant is null, it used the current time. Now, with this change, if someone passes a null instant to this method, it would cause a problem because instant.getMillis() would throw a NullPointerException.

That makes sense. So the mutant introduced a bug where passing a null instant would now cause an NPE instead of using the current time as a fallback.

Now looking at the tests that were affected:

The failing tests include TestMonthDay_Properties#testPropertyGetMonthOfYear which started failing but then was fixed by mutation. That might be unrelated or perhaps due to another change.

But more importantly, two tests that were passing before (TestDateTimeZone#testGetOffset_RI and testGetOffsetFixed_RI) are now failing because of the mutant. This suggests that these tests involve cases where getOffset is called with a null instant, expecting it to handle it correctly by using the current time.

So when the mutant changes line 329 to 'if (false)', it breaks this fallback mechanism. Hence, when getOffset is called with a null, instead of using the current time, it tries to call getMillis() on a null object, causing an NPE.

Therefore, the problem arises because the code now doesn't handle the case where instant is null correctly. The change introduced a regression in handling null values, leading to test failures.

So, considering this, the mutant caused a fault adjacent situation. It broke some tests that were previously passing, introducing new bugs related to how getOffset handles null instants.
</think>
